speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three and with Norbert Kerenyi.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I started to-- to see our paramilitary unit, I get into trouble with the-- the events of war much earlier than these German or Hungarian fascists. We were stationing in Csepel, Weiss Manfred factory. This was the largest ammunition and weapon factory in Central Europe, and as such it was a target for Ally air raids. And there was an Ally air raid just around July the 20th, in which a very large number-- in between 800 and 2,000 aeroplanes bombed this factory very effectively. They put the factory out of commission. Approximately 75%, 80% of them. The factory never could produce again the way as they did previously. But there was a very high price in human life. Particularly, Jewish paramilitary units were very heavily affected. The rule by the-- one of the fascist oriented chief executive officer of this factory was that in air raids, the Jews had to go out of the bunkers. There were bunkers built in such a fashion that they could resist even direct hits. I was participating in building one together with our unit, but they had the order that Jews have to leave-- when the air raids start-- these bunkers. And we were outside in the-- still, there were shelters. You know. Some shelters dug in the ground and covered with a few layer of bricks. But they were of course not resisting hits. In this single air raid that I mentioned to you, over 10,000 young Jewish men died. Altogether, approximately 20,000 people died in the area, and it was a major catastrophe in that sense. The fascist minister of interior who was in charge of this came out, and he really let this chief executive officer have it. He said that he exposed this-- he wasn't worried about the Jewish people dying, but he said that he has no labor force to send him. He said that you should take better care of forces than what you did, and he's not going to send any more people to him. He didn't have any more. They were all engaged in-- in other areas. But this heavily damaged our unit as well. My platoon had very heavy losses. And we were a very interesting unit. The number of people there was approximately 160, but the age was from 14 to 65, because they brought these people in from factories. The mix was very interesting, because there were people like me who tried to just survive, there were students, there were executives of factories who went into their factory, and there were Jewish workers. To my astonishment, the first time in my life I met these Jewish workers who were working in those factories. And some of the people who were there were very influential, and they managed to arrange with the ministry that our unit was transferred from this factory to Szob in a mine that produced stone in a stone mine. And Szob was outside of Budapest. It looked as a very innocent place. However, we were not very lucky with that, because Szob had a railway bridge which went through a river. And this railway bridge served the line to Berlin, to Germany. And the German army from the Balkans started to withdraw late in August. And military trains were coming practically continuously, leaving out 50, 100 meters in between two trains. And the Allied were bombing that bridge day and night. And fortunately unsuccessfully, because they hit the bridge many, many times, but did not hit the pillars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our unit need eventuality was ordered to fix the bridge full time whenever each and every air raid. They had a specially designated unit there, which was completely killed by a previous air raid. So they escaped in a large crater that was made by a previous large bomb, with the idea that once a bomb fell in a place, another one would not fall there. And this wasn't true. Another one fell there and this unit was completely wiped out. So we took up fixing the bridge. And we suffered further losses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now from there-- from Szob-- I took arbitrary leave several times. They called this with a Jewish or Yiddish word, [YIDDISH], which meant that I had to be back within 24 hours. If I am away for longer than 24 hours, this meant desertion, for which the punishment was death. If I am back within 48 hours, they called it arbitrary leave for which the punishment was one week solitary. So we didn't worry about that that much. And I managed to escape from this place many times to Budapest. And in the meantime, my brother, who was with another unit, was transferred to Budapest. He was in Budapest, but he was so law abiding that he refused to escape. And my father wanted to see him. He really loved him, and in order to satisfy this request, I-- from time to time when I escaped from Szob, I managed to travel to Budapest. I sneaked into his unit, I helped him out, and I stayed there until he came back, so the number count would fit. Because they counted numbers from time to time. They did not really worry too much about names. And this is how we lived in those days in Budapest. There was a constant fear of deporting Budapest. And just to characterize our frame of mind, one time there was a news coming to us to Szob that they are going to deport the population of Budapest. And my main concern was my younger brother. I know I told that I cannot do very much for my parents and for my sister, so I smuggled him out of Budapest and smuggled him into our units, which I considered safe. It wasn't, but I considered it safe. And he was there for a few days until this rumor died down. And then I smuggled him back to Budapest. This summer was a very long and hot summer in every way, and we managed to leave. The whole family was-- my older brother, and me with the military units, and the rest of my family in their own apartments-- in Budapest. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in several of my escapes, there was one last one, which already occurred from Csepel, because we were transferred back to Csepel from Szob. Finally they hit the bridge, and it was completely destroyed. We had no real fixing it, so they returned us to this factory. And at one of my escape, they discovered us. And they came to us-- they knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gendarmes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. These were members of the Hungarian army. Every labor unit like this was composed of about 160 to 200 members of the unit, and then to guard us and to lead us. There were 14, 15 soldiers who were-- they called it the frame. They were framing the unit. They were supervising us, and they discovered that we left. And in Csepel, this was a very strict military environment, and they were also probably afraid. What if they discovered this. We did not want to escape permanently, because they said that if we escaped they will hold our family responsible, and secondly, we had no place to go. So we returned. We knew that they discovered. And after our return, it was a very unpleasant Sunday afternoon, because there were six of us who returned voluntarily, including Tom [? Lantos, ?] who was with me in this military unit. And they decided to teach us a lesson, and they were beating us for about one and a half hours in one shift. And when they got tired, they stopped, and then they started it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they beat you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With their fists, with sticks, with their belts-- whatever they had. I distinctly remember that I discovered that Corporal [? Brannard ?] is so tight that he cannot really hit. And I told my friend Tom that let's go to him because he cannot hit anymore. So we were exposed to this until night. At night, they let us back to the sleeping place. Just illustrate you that we had a very strong camaraderie, around 10 o'clock they came to get us again. They read our name and somebody whose name I don't remember-- it was not a close friend, but a comrade-- said that you stay. We go down. And there were six people went down instead of us. And as soon as he came down at first, they hit him, and he fell, and his mouth-- he had a seizure. And there was froth coming out of his mouth. And this frightened them so much that they stopped the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we immediately went there to help him up. And we said, for heaven's sake, what happened to you? He said, nothing, you crazy, I just put some soap into my mouth to stop this nonsense. And this is how we lived in those days, which were relatively easy. Really nothing happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on October the 10th, I-- I was-- my feelings were hurt by this beating up. I didn't really like it at all and I decided that I discontinue my cooperation with the authorities, and we decided to leave. And we left on October the 10th. And first I was hiding in our apartment, in the place where-- that I already mentioned to you. And then October 15th, the Governor Horthy read his declaration instating that the real enemy are the Germans, and he ordered the Hungarian military units to attack the Germans. This did not really materialize in most places. But my brother's unit, under the leadership of their so-called [INAUDIBLE]-- the-- the soldiers-- you know, their commander made a speech and said that we didn't really treat you well, but our supreme leader ordered everybody-- every member of the army to attack the Germans. If you are willing to follow me, I will lead you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was at the moment with my brother, and we had very few weapons. They had the weapons. They were leading us. We just went there, and he spoke German. We surrounded a German military train in a neighboring railway station. And he told them that they are surrounded and they should surrender. And they did. So there was no exchange of any hostility. We picked up all their arms. We told them to go back to Germany, and left them on the train. And we went back to-- to this apartment house, which was an apartment house that was bombed and no longer suitable for tenants that this house, this military unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there until we found that units-- other military units with fascist paraphernalia and armbands are surrounding these units and the area. And this man, whose name I don't remember, gave me the speech-- that if you want, we shall resist them. He said our chances are nil, but at least we die for our country. And you decide. And they decided with a small majority that they'd rather survive. And at which point, using the same escape routes that I always used to sneak in and out to my brother over the roofs of houses-- apartment houses-- I didn't want to surrender with my brother, so we escaped. And he came with me. I managed-- he didn't want to go, but I managed to convince him. And we were home when the fascists took over. And third was a-- a-- really a final blow to the Jews of Budapest, because up until that time, they suffered very little losses. But the fascists take over-- eliminated Governor Horthy, and a man who was probably insane, medically insane took over. Colonel Szalasi, who became the supreme fascist leader of Hungary, and they started to deport the Jews of Budapest. As a first step, on October the 20th, they came to our-- no, the 18th-- they came to our apartment house that everybody from the age of 14 to 65 they will take away. And my younger brother was in that category. So my mother was very upset. And they knocked on the door where I was hiding that they are taking Ervin. And I told him that I and going with him, because I always felt that I could cope with these things. I had an extreme optimism that I will survive. As I told you, we were four boys and four girls in a group of people, and I bet with all the boys that I will survive. I believe it was $50 that I bet. And-- and I did survive, but I had nobody to collect from because they all died. And at any rate, I told that if my brother is going, I will be going with him. So we went to a football stadium where I found that they took my brother because it was an over zealous group who attacked our house, and they shouldn't have been taken. And they said that anybody who is under 16 can go home. So my brother could go home, but I was over 16, and I could not. And I very much disliked this idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to act very quickly as I had to do it several times in the remaining few months-- that I told my brother that he should give me-- we had two certificates-- documents with us. One of them was a personal document. The other one was a paramilitary document for everybody. And I asked for his paramilitary document, which showed that I am 14, and my name is Ervin Kohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him to-- we were 18 people lining up. I asked him to go out first and run in case if they discover me, that I am with the same name. But they didn't. I got out with his papers and he was sitting there waiting for me. So we just walked home. I don't think that too many people managed to escape from this place. And this was the first major removal of Jews from Budapest. Because they took these people to build fortifications for the defense of the Budapest. The Russian army was very close. And events were very fast then. My brother stayed home. My older brother return to his military unit. There was amnesty for those who returned. And he was lucky not to be with them during that period, but less lucky to return because they took this unit to Germany. And here it comes in the unfortunate fact that we had no idea what was happening in Germany. Indeed, the German propaganda was so good that an average person who was not a member of the Judenrat or had no other information told that to work for the Germans is a privileged situation. So we though that he will be all right, working for the Germans. And of course, this was not the case. He was taken to Buchenwald. The rest of the family stayed there, and it was a frightening episode in one of the few last days that we were in our own home. They came with [INAUDIBLE] to take all the Jews, by which time my mother had managed to get to the so-called Schutz-pass from the Swedes-- or the Swiss. And my younger brother said that we should go down. You know, I have the Schutz-pass. So-- because I told him we should hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said that why are you making a big issue out of nothing? I'm going down. And I told him not to, and I went into my usual hiding place. And about 10 minutes later, he was running up and told me that they are taking everybody, and they tear up the Schutz-passes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him to ask Michael, that is a friend of mine who was the son of the caretaker of the house-- ask him to close him into the camera where we kept food. And he close-- closed him in and put the door into his pocket. And then the fascists came to search their apartment, and he knew where Ervin is, but he had no idea that I am at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he led them right to my place, saying that this place looks suspicious to me, and just to remove attention from the place where my brother was hiding. And they told him, you are stupid. This is sealed by the military. So we-- the two of us escaped. They took everybody from the house but my mother went to the military command to send the Schutz-pass after my brother, which she could not do. So when she came back, the whole house was empty. And there was nobody in the apartment, so she really got into a panic.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we moved out, so she knew that we are there. My father did not come home. He was arrested by the fascists, and they gave him to the police. But there was a policeman who knew him, so he let him to go home on that night. And my sister arrived from first labor of the night, who also escaped. So the family was together again for that night. But we had to find a place. And we went to a Swiss house that was a house under the protection of the Swiss. And I found it unsafe. I felt that this is not a very good place. I heard that there is a place opposite to our house that they are building a hospital, or starting a hospital. And one of the nieces of my father, who was a doctor, lived in that house. So I decided I will try to get in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The commander of the house was the chief of-- the fascist chief of the Sixth District of Budapest. And of course I couldn't get in. There was somebody in the door in fascist uniform with a submachine gun, and he told that no one can get in. So I told him that if he lets me in, I'll pay him $1,000. He said that for that, he let's me in. And when I was going in, he asked me, what about the money? I said that when I am coming out. There is no other way to come out. And I managed to get in, and my aunt-- my father's niece-- arranged that we can move in. So I had the certificate from the commander of the house-- from this fascist chief-- that we can move in. So I was coming out, and he said that-- what about the money? And I told him that I had no $1,000. So we immediately became friendly on first name basis, because he told me that-- whatever-- then you are going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- and he lifted his submachine gun. But I told him that this would be foolish to do, because first of all I have $200 that I will give him right away. I said secondly, I am moving in. Here is the paper. And if he kills me, he gets into trouble with the commander because I am a member of the staff. But if I move in, I will immediately write him 10 IOUs, and every week I will cash one for him, because gradually I will the money. I thought that the Russians will be there in a week or two, so there will be no problem. But-- so he agreed that this is probably the best solution under the circumstances, and we moved in. And we lived there during the siege of Budapest. Everybody had a function there. My father was a night porter, my mother was in the laundry, my sister was in the kitchen, I became a member of a rescue team, and my younger brother was in charge of the central heating of the house, which meant that he kept feeding the coal-- it was based on coal-- into-- into this central heating equipment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is how we lived there when the siege came, not too long after we moved in. And the siege of Budapest was as bad as I expected it, but somehow I felt that we shall survive. At least I hoped so.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always concerned about my younger brother who could not really, in my opinion, take care of himself. So we were-- he was in the same team, initially, but I arranged that we had eight hours service, eight hours being readiness, and readiness-- and the eight hours sleep. And instead of him, I took the other eight hours because I knew that I can be more careful than he can. I don't know how you're careful during the siege, but I was, and I was successful. So he stayed in, which I think for which he is still grateful to this day. And by the way, the fact that I was right-- that he should not have gone down-- for many, many years made him to accept whatever I told him to do. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take another short break.
